steps.title: Support a universal ability
steps.content: The conclusion has two parts: participants can distinguish spontaneous from volitional laughter, and that ability appears across cultures. Supporting evidence must show above-chance recognition and similar performance across the societies studied, not just a pattern in one or two places.

steps.title: Reject narrower findings
steps.content: Choice A ties evaluations to economic conditions, which would make the ability vary with society rather than appear universal. Choice C describes acoustic variation in laughter, not whether listeners identify laugh types correctly. Choice D is limited to Austria and Samoa and does not show above-chance recognition across all 21 societies. The remaining finding gives both needed facts: correct judgments were better than chance, and recognition rates were similar across the societies. The answer is B.
